coredata.dc:title: Simple synthesis of Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite with different morphologies fabricated by tuning the synthesis parameters as novel antibacterial material
coredata.dc:creator: Teymourinia, Hakimeh
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Amiri, Omid
coredata.dc:description: Here we used Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) as a reducing agent to produce Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite from copper(II) acetate. Different morphologies of Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposites including particles, rod-like nanostructures, cauliflower-like nanostructures, and flower-like nanostructures were fabricated by tuning the synthesis parameters. Then the effect of these morphologies on the antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs against Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria was investigated. Results show that flower-like Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite shows the highest antibacterial activity with MIC of 0.1 against E. coli and 0.7 against Staphylococcus aureus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Cu2O/GQDs
GQDs
Cauliflower-like nanostructures and flower-like
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Studying the antibacterial effect of as-prepared nanosized materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of Cu2O/GQDs composite prepared in different solvent a) Ethanol, b) Water, and c) Acetone. Concentration of copper acetate, reaction time and temperature was 12 M, 20 min, and 70 C, respectively.
EDX of sample 1–6 which show the effect of Cu concentration and reaction time.
The effect of different concentration of copper acetate on the morphology of final products: a, b) 0.06 M (sample 1), c, d) 0.08 M (sample 2), e, f) 0.12 M (sample 3), and g, h) 0.14 M sample 8).
Cu2O/GQDs prepared with different reaction times: a, b) 10 min (sample 4), c, d). 40 min (sample 5).
The effect of reaction temperature on the morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composite: a, b) 25 C (sample 6) and c, d) 100 C (sample 7).
Effect of solvent on the morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composite: a, b) Aceton (sample 9) and c, d) Ethanol (sample 10).
TEM images for the as-synthesized flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures (sample 8).
MIC of sample 1–10 against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus.
Optical density for growing E. coli and Staphylococcus for flower like Cu2O/GQDs composite (sample 8).
Experimental detail for synthesis of Cu2O/GQDs composite.

head.ce:title: Simple synthesis of Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite with different morphologies fabricated by tuning the synthesis parameters as novel antibacterial material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, I. R. Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we used Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) as a reducing agent to produce Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite from copper(II) acetate. Different morphologies of Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposites including particles, rod-like nanostructures, cauliflower-like nanostructures, and flower-like nanostructures were fabricated by tuning the synthesis parameters. Then the effect of these morphologies on the antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs against Gram-negative (E. coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria was investigated. Results show that flower-like Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite shows the highest antibacterial activity with MIC of 0.1 against E. coli and 0.7 against Staphylococcus aureus.

ce:section.ce:para: There is an urgent need to develop a novel antibacterial material to destroy multi-drug resistant microorganisms due to complicated mechanisms associated with antibiotic resistance and emerging infectious diseases that are affecting global economies and public health. Nanomaterial due to its increased surface area compared to its bulk counterparts shed light on the antibiotic field [1–3]. Antibacterial properties of nanoparticles depend on the size, structure, and large surface of the nanometric particles. The majority of research in the antibacterial and antimicrobial field is focused on copper based materials and silver based materials. Silver nanoparticles could be effective in preventing infections, however, even if introduced more than a decade in the US, the use of silver has been infrequent in clinical practice due to cost implications [4,5].

Recently Cu-based NPs received great attention due to its low cost compared to Ag, the abundance of this metal, and excellent antibacterial activity against various Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and Gram-positive bacteria strains [6–9]. Cu-based NPs could destroy Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) and could be utilized for the treatment of surgical wounds, burns, and diabetic foot ulcer infections [10]. Copper shows low toxicity compared to other metals, that why it acts as co-factor for enzymes [11].

Up to now, several synthesis methods were used to obtain copper nanoparticles that could be classified into three main techniques: physical, chemical and biological [12–14]. Chemical reduction [15], sonochemical reduction [16], micro-emulsion (colloidal) [17], microwave-assisted [18], and hydrothermal syntheses [19] are the main chemical routes to synthesize copper nanoparticles. Biological method referee to the method that reducing copper ions by biomolecules present in fruits, plant extracts, marine algae, and bacteria [20–23]. Physical methods for preparation of copper nanoparticle are including laser ablation, pulsed wire discharge, pulsed wire discharge, and mechanical milling [24–26]. Physical methods allow producing copper nanoparticles with different sizes and morphologies but the process requires expensive equipment. In addition, the quality of the final product is lower than the nanoparticles produced by chemical methods.
Athinarayanan et al. and Kumar et al. reported that coupling Cu2O and CuO with graphene could improve the antibacterial activity of Cu2O and CuO [27,28].

In this research, we used GQDs as the reducing agent and as the second compound of Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite and controlled the growth and geometry of nanoparticles by adjusting the synthesis parameter. Different morphologies such as flower like, rod like and spherical copper particles were achieved.

To better understand the health and environmental impacts of this new nano-composite, we studied the antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs with different morphology against two bacterial models - Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus. Antibacterial results show that flower like Cu2O/GQDs (sample 8) shows higher antibacterial activity compare to the other morphologies. Flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures indicate promising low minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) against E Coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The MIC of 0.2 and 0.7 was achieved for Flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures, while it was 0.1 and 0.8 for Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures and 0.1 and 0.9 for Rod like Cu2O/GQDs nanoparticles against E Coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. In order to characterize as-prepared nanomaterials, Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were used. Based on these results, we introduce Cu2O/GQDs as a new antibacterial nanomaterials.

Detail for the synthesis of Graphene quantum dots could be found in our previous works [29,30]. Briefly, 0.25 g of Popcorn powder with 1–2 mm in diameters was added into 25 mL DI water, followed by stirring for 30 min. PH was 7. Finally, the solution was heated at 180 °C for 48 h into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave.

Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite was synthesized by using as-prepared GQDs as a reducing agent and second component of the composite. For this, 3 mL of as prepared GQDs solution was added to the 5 mL of a copper (II) acetate solution (0.12 M) and continuously stirred for 20 min. The temperature was adjusted at 70 C and the reaction time was 20 min. The reaction was done in natural PH. Detail of experimental could be founded in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the antibacterial effect of as-prepared nanosized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of different Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite was evaluated by microdilution assays, as described by the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI). Here E. coli and S. aureus were used as model microorganisms in order to measure the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of as-prepared composites. The strains of bacteria were cultivated in Mueller-Hinton broth incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The bacterial suspensions were diluted with Mueller-Hinton broth to OD570 = 0.2 and were distributed (100 μL) in a 96-well plate. Each well was exposed to different concentrations of nanomaterials (from 0.01 to 0.89. mg.L−1). The plates were incubated at 37 °C, and the bacterial growth was observed after 18 h. The test was repeated three times and the MIC was considered as the lowest nanomaterial concentration where no bacterial growth was observed.

Graphene quantum dots are tiny carbon-based particles with different functional groups that could reduce metal ions through two routes. Amine, hydroxide and the carboxylic acid group could chemically reduce Cu ions. Also, GQDs could absorb light and generate electron and hole pairs. These produced electrons could reduce Cu ions. In the present research, we applied these properties to reduce Cu and finally produce the Cu2O/GQDs composite. We also interestingly fabricate different morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composite by controlling the reaction parameters such as temperature, solvent, time and concentration.

We used Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) to study the effect of solvent, concentration, reaction time and temperature on the morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composites as final products. Four different concentration of Cu(OAc)2 were used to study the effect of precursor concentration on the morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composites (Fig. 3 a-h). Small and uniform particles with 20–40 nm in diameters were formed when the concentration of copper (II) acetate was 0.06 M (sample 1) (Fig. 3 a and b). By increasing the concentration of copper (II) acetate to 0.08 M (sample 2), rod like Cu2O/GQDs composites were achieved (Fig. 3 c and d). Fig. 3 e and f show the SEM images of Cu2O/GQDs composites when the concentration of Cu(OAc)2 was 12 M (sample 3). In this case, Cu2O/GQDs cauliflower-like nanostructures were formed. However, flower like nanostructures were fabricated by increasing the concentration of precursor to the 0.14 M (sample 8) (Fig. 3 g and h). Therefore it is possible to control the morphology from particles to flower like by tuning the concentration of copper (II) acetate. Reaction time is another parameter that could influence the morphology of Cu2O/GQDs composites. We have tested 10 (sample 4), 20 (sample 3) and 40 min (sample 5). As seen in Fig. 4 a and b, cauliflower-like nanostructures do not form yet when the reaction time was 10 min (sample 4). By changing the reaction time to 20 min, cauliflower-like nanostructures were formed (Fig. 3 e and f). When we gave 40 min to complete reaction (sample 5), more uniform Cu2O/GQDs cauliflower-like nanostructures were achieved. It seems we need at least 20 min to form cauliflower-like nanostructures.

We studied the effect of the reaction temperature as a parameter that could change the morphology of the final product (Cu2O/GQDs composite). Fig. 5 a and b show the morphology of the sample prepared at room temperature (sample 6). According to the SEM images, rod like Cu2O/GQDs composite was fabricated at room temperature. Cauliflower-like composite was formed by changing the reaction temperature to the 70 C (sample 3) (Fig. 3a and b). The possible reason is that rod like structures obtain enough kinetic energy to stuck together and form cauliflower-like nanostructures [31–36]. By increasing the temperature up to 100 C (sample 7), cauliflower-like nanostructures with diameters of 200–400 nm were formed (Fig. 5c and d). Also, we observed that these nanostructures stuck together somewhere. We figured out that doing the reaction in acetone as the solvent (sample 9) lead to form mixture of rod like and cauliflower-like nanostructures (Fig. 6 a and b). As illustrated in Fig. 6c and d, denser cauliflower-like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures were obtained when ethanol was used as the solvent (sample 10).

Fig. 7 a–c shows the different scale of TEM images for the as-synthesized flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures (sample 8). TEM images show that how GQDs and Cu2O form flower like structures. As indicated in TEM images, dark dots are GQDs, while bright parts are related to the Cu2O. The diameter of the GQDs is about 2–5 nm.

The MIC values of sample 1–10 which show the effect of morphology on the antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus are summarized in Fig. 8 and Table 2 . As seen from the table, we examined three concentrations for each Cu2O/GQDs composite. The concentrations of 0.075, 0.15, 0.30 and 0.70, 0.75, 0.90 of sample 1 were tested to figure out the MIC against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively.

Sample 1, 2, 3 and 8 show the effect of concentration of precursor and the morphology and antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures. According to Fig. 8 and Table 2, MIC against E. coli was 0.3, 0.2, 0.1 and 0.1, respectively. Related MIC against Staphylococcus aureus was 0.9, 0.8, 0.8 and 0.7, respectively. MIC for sample 4 and 5 which show the effect of reaction time was 0.1 against E. coli and 0.7 against Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. Sample 6 and 7 reflexed the effect of reaction temperature on the antibacterial activity of Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures. Since the morphology of sample 6 and 7 are close, they show the same MIC against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. Based on the results presented in Table 2 and Fig. 8, flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures (sample 8) show the highest antibacterial activity (lowest MIC). This MIC is much lower than the 100 and 1000 reported for the MICs of E. coli. Fig. 9 shows the optical density for growing E. coli and Staphylococcus for the flower like Cu2O/GQDs nanostructures (sample 8). The optical density of bacterial growth for E. coli and Staphylococcus were decreased over time which shows the reduction of bacterial growth rate.

In this research, we used as-synthesized GQDs to reduce Cu(II) ions and produce Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite. Then we studied the effect of concentration of Cu precursor, solvent, reaction time and temperature on the morphology and antibacterial activity of as-synthesized Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite. We fabricate a variety of morphology such as particles, rod-like nanostructures, cauliflower-like nanostructures, and flower-like nanostructures by tuning the synthesis parameters. Finally, we study the antibacterial activity of the different Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite with morphologies against E. coli and Staphylococcus as Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, respectively. Results show that flower-like Cu2O/GQDs nanocomposite shows the highest antibacterial activity. Its MIC was 0.1 against E. coli and 0.7 against Staphylococcus aureus. Products were characterized by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/122).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xu, W. Wang, W. Zhu, Shape Evolution and Size-Controllable Synthesis of Cu2O Octahedra and Their Morphology-Dependent Photocatalytic Properties, J. Phys. Chem. B, 110 (2006) 13829-13834.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Low temperature synthesis of flowerlike ZnO nanostructures by cetyltrimethylammonium bromide-assisted hydrothermal process
J Phys Chem B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhang, D. Yang, Y. Ji, X. Ma, J. Xu, D. Que, Low Temperature Synthesis of Flowerlike ZnO Nanostructures by Cetyltrimethylammonium Bromide-Assisted Hydrothermal Process, J. Phys. Chem. B, 108 (2004) 3955-3958.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanism and modeling of nanorod formation from nanodots
Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ethayaraja, R. Bandyopadhyaya, Mechanism and Modeling of Nanorod Formation from Nanodots, Langmuir 23 (2007) 6418-6423.
